podcasts.name: Moody's Podcasts
podcasts.title: Supply chain risk 101 | Back to basics
podcasts.excerpt: Supply chain risk has moved from a back-office concern to a boardroom priority. In this episode of Risk Reframed, we dig into the foundations to understand what supply chain risk management really means in today’s interconnected world. Carolina Azar , Senior Director and Lead Strategist for Supply Chain Risk Management at Moody’s, joins host, Alex Pillow , to break down the fundamentals and explain why the discipline has rapidly evolved over recent years. Their conversation explores how modern supply chain risk management goes beyond third‑party due diligence and supplier onboarding to deliver resilience, foresight, and enterprise‑wide decision support. Key topics discussed include: What supply chain risk management is today, and how it differs from traditional third‑party due diligence Why risks can originate anywhere in the supply network, from sub‑tier suppliers to logistics and can be impacted by everything from geopolitics to extreme weather events The essential building blocks of an effective supply chain risk program, including mastered data, risk assessments, workflows, and timely reporting How supply chain risk management is becoming a strategic input into enterprise decision‑making, including market entry, capital allocation, and M&A The growing role of technology and AI, alongside the continued importance of human judgment, in managing uncertainty and resilience Additional resources: Supply and command video Moody’s blog posts on supplier risk Supply chain in the spotlight | Risk Reframed episode To learn more about Moody’s please visit our website or get in touch ; we would love to hear from you. Hosted by Simplecast, an AdsWizz company. See pcm.adswizz.com for information about our collection and use of personal data for advertising.
podcasts.transcript: By downloading or listening to this podcast, you are agreeing to Moody's legal terms and conditions found@moody's.com disclaimer including that the information provided is not investment or financial advice and that Moody's will not be liable for losses arising from your use of the information. This is Risk Reframed the show where we you the leading risk experts from Moody's, our partners and the wider ecosystem to discuss the risks organizations face, how they're interconnected, and ultimately what you, the risk professional, can do about them. Supply chain risk management has come into increasing focus over the last few years. But supply chain risk procurement, third party risk management, where does it all fit together? To help me unpack this, I'm joined by Carolina Azar, senior director and lead strategist for Supply Chain Risk Management at Moody's. Carolina, how are you? I'm doing good, Alex, how are you? Yeah, very well. I'm always delighted when someone makes the trip over from our US office. It makes me feel very important and lets us use this fantastic studio that Mark set up. So I appreciate it. And supply chain risk has been something that's kind of been dominating my my newsfeed, to be honest, the last sort of 18 months. Part of it because I've trained my algorithm and it's echo chambering me back because after trying to learn more and more about it and we had a couple of podcasts last year from Supply Chain now host Scott Luton, which was great just to kind of start to touch on the subject. But I think for a lot of our listeners, particularly those that have come from that KYC background that we've traditionally focused on, and some of our internal listeners who might know a lot about credit, for instance, but not necessarily this area. It feels like we need to get the ground in right and make sure that as we talk more and more about supply chain in your area of expertise, that yeah, as I say, we have the basics covered. So with that, let me ask the foundational question, what is supply chain risk management? Happy to answer that because it has been changing over the years. The market has changed since COVID and the definition has also evolved. So today supply chain risk management is all about identifying, assessing, mitigating and continuously monitoring risks across the entire supply network. What I think the prior definition missed is that disruptions or challenges can happen at any point. It could be from raw materials and sub tier supplier, through logistics, operations, final delivery. So it spans across physical, digital, financial, regulatory and geopolitical risk domains rather than focusing only on what the prior definition did which what was about the surface cost reduction and screening entities just, you know, are they real or not? But that ended there. Okay, so before there was a. Just do the basics, tick the box. Yes, this company's real. There's no obvious sanction or reputational risk perhaps. And that, and then the cost reduction, that's sort of that procurement or traditional procurement play. But you're saying it's changed now and it's looking from end to end. Where did everything that came to this point of this supplier give me this thing that I've asked for on paying for? Now you need to be aware of that whole process. Yes, so. So maybe a very, I think clear and succinct explanation is third party due diligence can answer the question, can I do business with this entity? Yes, you can, but it's at your own risk. The entity is a real entity. It has been cleared for doing business with. Sure you can. But then that set, then the prior definition of supplier risk was about or what it is today. Supplier risk is about what is my risk exposure if I do business with this entity? And then of course from there, how do I kind of reduce my risk exposure? But the definition today of supply chain risk is not only number one and two, which is that cover. Can I do business with this company? Yes, you can. What is my risk exposure? And then it goes one step further around where could our supply chain break and what would it cost us if it does? So where does it break? And can I try to put certain, you know, governance and processes in place to react quickly and try to diminish the lost in revenue, the means, deliverables, can I maneuver? Okay, we'll come on to how that's, that's evolved. But sounds like you have to have one and two, right? Yes. Be compliant, make sure you know that surface level before you then go deeper. But it's that depth and breadth that we're going to get into. One thing, Caroline, that always impresses me about yourself is like the passion with which you speak on this subject and of, you know, advocated for it with myself and others in the business. Why is it that you're so passionate about supply chain risk specifically? I can tell you a couple of things, so a couple of buzzwords that have you might have heard of. It's all about, you know, building resilience, scenario planning, decision making for planning for reactive to disruptions. So if you think about all that, it's a new normal. It's not about, oh, can we become more resilient just around climate Risk. Can we become a little bit more resilient around geopolitical risk? It's everything coming at our clients at once and how do we help them? And we have so much data, so many analytics, so much intelligence that we can help with that. That's why I am so passionate. Like how can't we miss this opportunity to help clients when everything is hitting them from right to left? So a couple of things to have in mind about why am I so passionate about it? Supply chain risk management today is not about one bad supplier. It's about how risk cascades across the entire network and then disruptions start anywhere. As I just mentioned, it could be at the subsupplier, at the single factory, at the port, a logistic leg, it doesn't matter. And then the impact is not just theoretical. The impact is huge. Is revenue at risk, is missed deliverables, is lost customers and millions of dollars wipe off of ebitda. So a good supply chain risk management process connects all the dots and it shows you how your supply chain is wired, where the real choke points are and what happen if one of them falls. So it's about foresight more than just risk awareness. I think we passed that point. Risk awareness identification is a must have. But then what? And it's all about that foresight and the difference between reacting to disruptions as opposed to creating smarting, sourcing and inventory in order to adapt to disruptions. Sounds like the problem itself or the challenge for the clients themselves is what's spurring you on. So that desire to be, what do we call it, being customer obsessed is the official. Well, it's a good challenge, unfortunately for our clients. Customer obsessed. There's the challenge and then maybe you're something of a problem solver so you want to go and tackle the hardest, most complex. Give me a challenge and I love it. Very good. And is that typical of your supply chain professional? I mean, does every company have a supply chain risk team? No. Or yeah. Well, tell me, how is it normally set up or is it spread across departments in your experience? It depends on the size of the company. The smaller the company, the more blurred the functions are. And typically maybe, you know, the compliance department does a little bit of the, you know, supplier risk assessment or the procurement department does a little bit of the compliance assessment and due diligence. The bigger and the more, you know, sophisticated the company gets, then the lanes are way more structured and you know, you start with the legal or compliance for the pre onboarding risk assessment, then you go into the procurement or Supply chain teams for the onboarding and continuous monitoring which is an interesting step into where the market is going. And then the companies that do have a supply chain or operation strategy team, it's becoming more like a enterprise wide function as opposed to a reporting function. Okay. So again the most sophisticated, the biggest, what we call strategic clients now the supply chain risk management function has become so central to business continuity, financial stability and competitive advantage that they are becoming some sort of, you know, expert function across the business. So you just don't want those silos and we speak about silos a lot on this show because many risk, parts of risk, risk types get stuck in a silo and then bemoan the lack of, you know, either compliance or impact they can have across their business. Whereas it sounds like what you're saying is this is so important that you need cross functional expertise comes, comes together and the larger the org, the more, more that's realized. And I suppose the, the thing I'm sort of thinking for is there's with any type of risk or any type of operation, there's always different companies at different stages of maturity. For those that are at the start of this or somewhat basic, what, what are the absolute basic tenants, what are like the must to actually be able to say you are doing supply chain risk management versus just say third party due diligence? Well it depends on where they are also in, in their journey towards digitalizing the entire, you know, third party risk management process. But, but I would say, I would say let's start with data. Data can come directly from your vendors, your suppliers. Then data could come from third party risk management vendors. Data can also be search. So let's start with combining. For those companies that are trying to start with supplier risk management, let's try to gather as much data as you can about a company. And then once that would be step number one and then number two of course is what type of insight can I draw from this data? What can this data tell me? And then if there's gaps then find a way to remediate that gap. Where do you need information and go and get it. But if you need to assess a supplier's performance and ability to withstand the shock, we are talking about not only financial data, operational data, regulatory compliance, reputational, esg, sustainability, it, cyber, I mean the list goes on. So the more information you can get about the companies that you are doing business with or your clients are doing business with, the better. And then we need to draw insights in terms of historical backward looking, in terms of trying to assess past performance and then try to determine what would be the behavior moving forward that's at the foundation. Okay, so let me just recap that because I think you know so much and I'm constantly putting myself in the shoes of the person that maybe is just coming to this topic the first time. So, first step, make sure you have the data sources, both from the vendors, but also from external sources that's combined. Then define what risk insights you need, whether they be, as you said, cyber risk, it could be sustainability risks, it could be environmental, it could be compliance, it could be something else. And then this next piece is sort of how do you monitor, how do you make sure that the ongoing performance monitoring and disruption monitoring is there? Because otherwise you're still not really doing supply chain risk management. You are just assessing your suppliers. Is that fair recap of the first three steps? Well, I didn't get to the second. So the first is the data. You're absolutely right. And then what do you do with that data? So you need to find a way to help assess the overall risk of doing business with that entity. And you can do that manually or you can do that in an automated way. You can. Then once you have that risk assessment workflow or scorecard, then you need to be able to provide your clients with the ability to use it in order to make an onboarding decision, yes or no. And then once a vendor or supplier has been onboarded, then you have to continuously monitor until the end of the life cycle. So you need risk assessment workflows and then monitoring workflows. And then I think something that is extremely important in third party risk management or supply risk or supply chain risk management is dashboards. Any, any flags, any, any challenges, any impact, anything that can disrupt the business needs to be monitored in real time. And red flags are extremely important because then that's what these companies need in order to react quickly and assess next steps. So if you don't have a nice dashboard that can alert you to something that just happened across your portfolio of suppliers, then you will not be able to react quickly enough. Yeah, I'm reminded of the cliche, right? What gets measured gets managed. And if it's not in the report and it's not being seen, it might exist somewhere in some text or some questionnaire, but if it's not flagged up, then it won't actually get sorted, it won't get managed, as the saying goes. Yeah, it's not about anymore sending you an email and saying, can you look into this? So, so if I Think of a supplier risk and then we can talk about supply chain. But supply risk management tool, if it's a triangle at the bottom is the data, then the next layer are the insights and the risk assessments and then the top of the pyramid would be all those workflows. And how can you visualize in your entire portfolio to quickly assess where there are challenges and issues that you need to react? Yeah, I like that framing. We should, if we don't already, we should make that as a poster. You mentioned that it obviously things have changed a lot in the last several years. Right. And I think we touched on this with Scott in the podcast last year. But I hear a lot of supply chain people talk about COVID changed everything. And obviously we know know since then there's been many other disruptions to the world. I won't go through them all. But how have you seen things change and evolve over this last five, six years or so? Absolutely so. And it's one of the things that going back to the topic about passion, it has been a function that has changed so much. Maybe the function that has changed the Most in the 30 years that I have been a professional. So first of all, and now speaking specifically about supply chain risk management, not supply risk so much, but supply chain risk management, it's becoming a strategic input into everything else that an organization does is not just a reporting function anymore at all. We expect supply chain risk management function to influence where companies compete, not just how they source market entry and exit decisions, capital allocation, mergers and acquisitions and the overall company enterprise wide strategy. So it's unbelievable to see that happening already. So sort of the political clout, I suppose of this function is just growing quite exponentially, tremendously. Because if you think about it, because it includes so many different dimensions of risk that if you are already covering them and you are already making a judgment around where you draw the line with doing business or not with certain critical providers, then why not to use the same risk assessment or the same depth of data to inform what should we do as a company in terms of doing business as a company? Where do we exit the market? Where do we entry market? What about, you know, a merger, an acquisition, investment decisions? I mean it's really becoming that sort of, you know, a strategic input in the decision making of a complete of an entire enterprise. Wow. Carolee, this is why I talked about your passion. Right. Anything else that we should think about when we're talking about this evolution and the, the changes around this function? Few more things. So we just talked about the supply Chain risk management expertise becoming a strategic input in the overall enterprise decision making. But also another one that I would like to talk about is that it has predominantly be around can we predict but I think it's about now becoming more preemptive. So the human judgment remains, but the systems are increasingly prestaging the response and we need to talk about how technology and specifically AI is going to impact that judgment, that decision making, that response planning. So that's another one which is still to be seen in my mind. How will technology and AI support that being predictive and making sure that the response planning and the decision making, maybe we can help automate it a little bit more. But then at the top that human judgment needs to remain. And now you are going to see a lot of. You have been reading a lot about talent and talent acquisition in supply chain risk management. It has to do with that, with that top of pyramid decision making, human judgment that needs to remain. Yeah. So if we think about that pyramid again, even if you've got great data and some of it from AI or some of it, you know, sourced wherever it's coming from and then you've got your risk insights and analytics and you've got your workflows, your automation and you've maybe got some AI agents or many of them doing specific things. However, if a wrong decision is made, it's still going to fall to the person. So I think what you're saying is very consistent what we're hearing in other risk types, which is that you must have that human in the loop and constantly training these agents and retraining them to make sure they don't miss that nuance, don't miss that gut feeling that maybe they're going yeah, the data told me this, but that data seems wrong or I just don't have the right feeling around it. So yeah, I can understand why there'd be a premium on getting these top experts on particularly those that have been through the last, as we say six plus years where they've really had to, yeah, change and, or change how they apply their skill sets. So it's, yeah a fascinating area. I mean with customers that you're working with today, where do you sort of. Are they all about the agents now, these leading customers or is it more about still getting the good basics in so they can get to that point? Do you have a. Well, I suppose it could be a mix. Right. You have different customers at different, different points. But so it's interesting, it's because we deal with smaller clients, medium sized clients and Then of course, you know, top tier clients, it depends. But let me maybe start with the ones that are already ahead in terms of switching from third party risk management to supplier risk. And now getting into the supply chain space, I can talk a little bit about a few things. So they are now understanding that disruptions are, are chronic. It's not just happening once or twice. So then it's all about, you know, it's not just about disruptions, it's about governing uncertainty. And I love that. I don't think anyone can claim today that they know how to price uncertainty and assess, you know, if you have become resilient enough. But that's where it's going. So I think that we already passed the point of can I identify when something happens? If, if you still don't have that in place, you are so far behind. So these companies now have systems in place that help them identify when something is happening across the network. There is a missing link or a broken link. Something has happened either internally coming from something happening across the network, across the supplier network, but also an external shock. So either intrinsically or not or external, I think that the point of identifying that something happened has already passed. And it's that point around, can we prize resiliency? How do we become resilient around going back to those sourcing decisions? Instead of sourcing for cost, sourcing for proximity, sourcing for of course the quality of goods. It's about can I source for a company that is demonstrating or has demonstrated that their performance has been robust enough and they have everything required in place to withstand an internal and an external shock. I think I see what you're saying when you say sort of price this in. So there's the price you pay for a more reliable product, there's the price you're going to charge yourself based on the vendors and their vendors that they choose and so on. And also there's an insurance element when you say price this right, because you might take insurance policies around that supply chain for that product. And our insurance colleagues are incredibly interested in this depth and breadth, the supplier, supplier, supplier and so on that makes up this network. So it's a very interesting choice of language there around pricing this in. I think resilience is becoming a commodity in the market. I think that this is going to become a question when trying to do business with another company around how you be able to build resilience across your supply chain. But I think it's beyond the supply chain because you also need to be resilient or create a Very resilient stack of clients portfolio as well as suppliers portfolio. And it's about okay, doing business with this portfolio of clients, doing business with these portfolio of suppliers. Can I rely on them? Will I be able to continue the business five, ten years from now? What does it mean? And then maybe helping them also grow as companies and grow in terms of become stronger in their internal systems, in their governance, in their policies, in their practices. I think it's becoming more a conversation between companies a lot less transactional. Less transactional and more about. It's like, I don't know, going to the doctor and you only, you know, show your, your blood work. This is going beyond that. Well, I want to see all your X rays for the last 10 years. Yeah, yeah, yeah. You want to know the medical history? You want to know what your lifestyle is? Yeah, I. That's right. Do you joke to your success? I love that. Exactly. So. So I think resilience will increasingly be measured, compared and priced by the market and becoming a decision point when doing business across companies especially the bigger or the more sophisticated you are. Yes, the more advanced. Yeah, we've covered a lot of ground really quickly and I'm conscious that a lot of our audience haven't come from a supply chain background. So I want to leave more for another day. If people want to just jump in right now, then, you know, read something else, watch something else. Is there any way you'd send them to keep going down the supply chain rabbit hole, as it were? Well, so we did a very interesting and topical supply and command video on the Moody's YouTube channel. But also, I mean, if you are interested, you're passionate about supply chain risk. There are a lot of reading material, books, literature around supply chain risk management, about the supply chain risk handbook, outsourcing and resilience guidance, supply chain risk management automation, I mean, you name it. There's a lot of material out there and it's interesting to see how now the topic of resiliency is coming and how companies are now trying to adapt pretty much their practices, their policies, their systems in order to become more robust and resilient to intrinsic internal and external shocks. As I say, there's a lot there. So we'll certainly get the supply and command. Another Mark Rundle and team special from our comms group and then maybe we'll have a Carolina's top three resources in the show notes. If people want to check those out and learn a bit more, they're there for you. And until then, we'll just say thank you Carolina for coming in. Until next time. Thanks to the marketing team for constantly pulling all this together, and to Mark and team for the production and editing. Really appreciate it. Thanks for listening to this Moody's Talks podcast. To find out more about the topics discussed, please follow the links in the show notes. You can check out other Moody's Talks podcasts by visiting Moody's.com podcasts.
